summary: The week of July 13–19 was dominated by the escalating U.S.-Iran conflict over the Strait of Hormuz, which persisted daily with airstrikes and retaliations. The U.S. launched strikes for eight consecutive nights, targeting Iranian bases, bridges, and infrastructure, while Iran retaliated against Gulf allies. By July 16, the death toll exceeded 35, and by July 19, U.S. troop deaths in Jordan triggered further bombing. In Ukraine, a Russian strike killed three in Odesa on July 15 as EU Chief visited Kyiv for defense talks. Protests erupted on July 16 after President Zelensky removed the popular defence minister. On July 19, Russia launched its largest ballistic missile attack on Kyiv following a Ukrainian drone strike. In Bangkok, a bar fire on July 13 killed at least 27, rising to 30 by July 14 as police probed negligence. China purged a third Politburo member in an anti-graft campaign on July 14. France approved an assisted dying bill for adults with incurable illnesses on July 15. On July 17, 23 pupils were feared dead after a school bus crash in Uganda. The UN reported over 500 Rohingya feared dead after two boats capsized on July 16. Politically, Andy Burnham was set to become UK Prime Minister on July 20 after winning Labour leadership on July 18. Stories that faded quickly included the initial U.S.-Iran attacks (July 13), the Bangkok bar fire (July 13), the death of U.S. senator Lindsey Graham (not dated, but listed as faded), China joining the U.S. in reusable orbital rocket capability (not dated), and the link between UK heatwaves and over 2,700 deaths (not dated). These events appeared only briefly and did not develop further.
fadedStories: U.S. and Iran Trade New Rounds of Attacks in Fight Over Strait of Hormuz
